title: Boris Cherny on Claude Code Setup with Dan Abramov, Harper Reed & More | Mark Curphey posted on the topic | LinkedIn
author: Mark Curphey
text:
Boris Cherny, the original developer of Claude Code, posted about his development setup on X recently. It went viral — over 6 million views. That triggered me to do some research. And I realised my biggest learning this week? The `/init` command. Sometimes I'm embarrassed at such basics that I've missed. But honestly, it shows the complexity and fast learning curve of all this AI tooling. So I dug in. Which developers and projects actually use CLAUDE.md files? **The results surprised me.** --- **Influential Developers:** - **Dan Abramov** (React co-creator) — uses it on overreacted.io - **Harper Reed** (Obama 2012 CTO) — wrote extensively about his Claude Code workflow - **Jesse Vincent** (Perl 6 project lead, K-9 Mail creator) — pioneered the "Superpowers" plugin - **Simon Willison** (Django co-creator, Datasette) — landed 259 PRs in 30 days using Claude Code - **Tanner Linsley** (TanStack/React Query creator) — publicly using Claude Code + Opus 4.5 **Companies:** - Stripe - Cloudflare - Vercel - Supabase - Sentry There are now 70+ documented examples across TypeScript, Python, Rust, Go, and Swift in the **awesome-claude-md** repo — a curated collection of high-quality CLAUDE.md files from leading open-source projects. It's a great resource to learn from and get ideas for structuring your own, without affecting your current project. --- **So what is CLAUDE.md?** It's essentially your project's "memory file" for Claude Code. When Claude opens your repo, it reads this file first—giving it instant context about your codebase. **Setting it up takes 2 minutes:** ``` cd your-project claude /init ``` Claude scans your package files, existing docs, config files, and code structure. Then generates a tailored CLAUDE.md with build commands, test instructions, and coding conventions. --- **What I've learned should go in your CLAUDE.md:** Keep it under 300 lines. I now include: - Build/test commands - Key directories - Coding conventions - Branch naming rules - What NOT to do (the gotchas I've hit) I've also started using `#` while chatting to add notes to CLAUDE.md on the fly. Over time, it becomes a living document that reflects how I actually work. --- **The mental shift for me:** I've stopped thinking of AI coding tools as autocomplete. Now I think of them as a junior dev who needs good onboarding. Would I throw a new hire into my codebase with zero documentation? CLAUDE.md is that documentation—except it loads in milliseconds at the start of every session. --- Still learning. If you've got tips, I'd love to hear them.

Here are 13 Claude Code setup tips from the creator himself, Boris Cherny. This is an incredible resource, I highly recommend referencing it to level up your own workflow! I’ve made it concise to fit on LinkedIn; for the full thread, go to: https://lnkd.in/gehbGWDA 1. Terminal Parallelism: "I run 5 Claudes in parallel in my terminal. I number my tabs 1-5 and use system notifications to know when a Claude needs input." 2. Web Parallelism: "I also run 5-10 Claudes on claude.ai/code... I hand off local sessions to web (using &) or manually kick off sessions in Chrome and --teleport back and forth." 3. Model Choice: "I use Opus 4.5 with thinking for everything. Even though it's slower than Sonnet, you steer it less and it's better at tool use, so it is almost always faster in the end." 4. Shared Context: "Our team shares a single CLAUDE.md. Anytime we see Claude do something incorrectly, we add it to the file so Claude knows not to do it next time." 5. PR Integration: "I often tag @.claude on coworkers' PRs to add to the CLAUDE.md. We use the Claude Code Github action for this—it's our version of Compounding Engineering." 6. Plan Mode: "Most sessions start in Plan mode (shift+tab twice). I go back and forth with Claude until I like its plan, then switch to auto-accept mode so Claude can 1-shot it." 7. Slash Commands: "I use slash commands for every 'inner loop' workflow. These save me from repeated prompting and are checked into git in .claude/commands/." 8. Subagents: "I use subagents like code-simplifier and verify-app. I think of subagents as automating the most common workflows I do for most PRs." 9. Formatting Hooks: "We use a PostToolUse hook to format Claude's code. It handles the last 10% to avoid formatting errors in CI later." 10. Smart Permissions: "I use /permissions to pre-allow safe bash commands. Most of these are checked into .claude/settings.json to avoid unnecessary prompts." 11. Tooling (MCP): "Claude Code uses all my tools: posts to Slack (via MCP), runs BigQuery queries, and grabs logs from Sentry. Config is shared via .mcp.json." 12. Long-Running Tasks: "(a) prompt Claude to verify its work with a background agent when it's done, (b) use an agent Stop hook to do that more deterministically, or (c) use the ralph-wiggum plugin. I will also use either --permission-mode=dontAsk or --dangerously-skip-permissions in a sandbox to avoid permission prompts for the session, so Claude can cook without being blocked on me." 13. Verification: "Give Claude a way to verify its work. This is the most important thing. If Claude has that feedback loop (tests or UI checks), it will 2-3x the quality of the result."

🚀 LeetCode Daily Challenge 🧩 Problem: Minimize Maximum Pair Sum in Array You are given an integer array nums of even length. You must pair up all elements such that each element is used exactly once. 🎯 Goal: Minimize the maximum pair sum among all possible pairings. 🧠 Key Intuition This is a classic greedy + sorting problem: To keep the maximum pair sum as small as possible, Pair the smallest element with the largest element This balances the sums and avoids creating a very large pair Key observations: Sorting the array helps in making optimal pairings Pairing extremes minimizes the worst-case sum Any other pairing will only increase the maximum sum 🛠 Approach Sort the array Initialize two pointers: One from the start (smallest) One from the end (largest) For each pair: Compute nums[i] + nums[n - i - 1] Track the maximum among these sums Return the maximum pair sum obtained 🧪 Why this works Greedy pairing ensures balance Sorting guarantees optimal global pairing Simple and efficient strategy 📈 Complexity Analysis Time Complexity: O(n log n) Space Complexity: O(1) 🔗 Problem https://lnkd.in/dT4QZynd 💻 Solution https://lnkd.in/d2wGs2HE #LeetCode #Cpp #DSA #Greedy #Sorting #Arrays #ProblemSolving #CodingChallenge #LeetCodeDailyChallenge #Algorithms

Hello connection's !!! I’m excited to share a hands-on learning experience where I explored one of Java’s lesser-discussed but powerful features — Integer Cache and JVM memory management. What started as a classroom explanation turned into a practical experiment to truly understand how Java optimizes memory internally. Project Overview This project is a custom simulation of Java’s Integer Cache mechanism. Using a fixed range of -128 to 127, I implemented my own Integer Cache using an array to demonstrate how Java avoids creating new objects for frequently used integer values. The program takes user input, checks whether the value already exists in the cache, and explains how object references are reused or newly created. Technical Highlights Defined explicit minimum and maximum cache limits to match JVM behavior Used an array-based cache to simulate how Integer objects are stored internally Implemented logic to detect whether a value already exists in the cache Demonstrated how new objects are created only when needed Explained the interaction between Java Stack (references) and Heap (objects) Used BufferedReader for controlled input to closely observe runtime behavior Learning Impact This project helped me understand how Java works beyond syntax — especially how memory optimization, object reuse, and references operate at runtime. Writing this logic myself gave me clarity on why Integer caching exists and how the JVM efficiently manages memory under the hood. It also strengthened my confidence in reading and reasoning about Java source-level concepts. Writing code is important — but understanding how the JVM executes it is what truly builds a strong backend developer. The link for the source code is : https://lnkd.in/djfqYXkA Hope You Enjoy. Concepts Used : BufferedReader Arrays for each loops Global variables Technology Used: JAVA Tools Used: terminal OBS(for screen recording) Inspired By: Yash Sawalkar sir . https://lnkd.in/dZJnd6gt #Integer_Cache #java #JVM #arrays #array
Integer_Cache !!!!

🚀 LeetCode Daily Challenge 🧩 Problem: Minimum Pair Removal to Sort Array I You are given an integer array nums. In one operation, you can remove two adjacent elements and replace them with their sum. 🎯 Goal: Find the minimum number of operations required to make the array non-decreasing. 🧠 Key Intuition This problem is all about greedy decisions: If the array is already sorted, no operations are needed Otherwise, we should reduce disorder as efficiently as possible Merging the adjacent pair with the minimum sum is the best local choice Smaller merged values are less likely to break the sorted order later 🛠 Approach First, check if the array is already non-decreasing → return 0 While the array is not sorted: Find the adjacent pair with the minimum sum Replace that pair with their sum Count the operation Repeat until the array becomes non-decreasing 🧪 Why this works Greedily removing the smallest adjacent pair minimizes disruption Each operation strictly reduces the array size Guarantees convergence to a sorted array 📈 Complexity Analysis Time Complexity: O(n²) Space Complexity: O(n) 🔗 Problem https://lnkd.in/dTyEsBcn 💻 Solution https://lnkd.in/djA3XzW6 #LeetCode #Cpp #DSA #Greedy #Arrays #ProblemSolving #CodingChallenge #LeetCodeDailyChallenge #Algorithms

🚀 LeetCode Day 41 📘 Problem: 744. Find Smallest Letter Greater Than Target Today’s problem was about binary search on characters – a small twist but a great concept refresher! 💡 Key Idea We need to find the smallest letter strictly greater than the target. If the target is greater than or equal to the last letter, we wrap around to the first letter. 🧠 Approach ✅ Use Binary Search ✅ Compare characters ✅ Handle wrap-around case ✅ Time Complexity: O(log n) ✅ Space Complexity: O(1)

🚀 LeetCode Daily Challenge 🧩 Problem: Divide an Array Into Subarrays With Minimum Cost I You are given an integer array nums. 🎯 Goal: Divide the array into three subarrays such that: The first subarray contains only nums[0] The cost is defined as the sum of the first elements of all three subarrays Return the minimum possible cost. 🧠 Key Intuition The problem is based on a simple greedy observation: The first subarray is fixed → it must contribute nums[0] To minimize the total cost, we should choose the two smallest possible values from the remaining elements as the starting elements of the other two subarrays Key observations: Only the first elements of subarrays affect the cost Sorting the remaining elements helps us easily pick the smallest two No complex partitioning is required 🛠 Approach Store nums[0] as part of the answer Sort the subarray nums[1 … n-1] Add the two smallest elements from the sorted part Return the total sum 📈 Complexity Analysis Time Complexity: O(n log n) Space Complexity: O(1) 🔗 Problem https://lnkd.in/dTWT-jac 💻 Solution https://lnkd.in/di7h24PJ #LeetCode #Cpp #DSA #Greedy #Sorting #Arrays #ProblemSolving #CodingChallenge #LeetCodeDailyChallenge #Algorithms

Insight: When you find yourself writing similar code in different places, the overlap is begging you to extract the underlying structure. This is why the DRY principle works. it's a special case of "the overlap reveals structure."

📌 LeetCode 100 Days Challenge – Day 31 Solved 54. Spiral Matrix today! ✅ Problem Summary: Given a 2D matrix, return all elements in spiral order, starting from the top-left corner and moving clockwise. Approach: Maintain four boundaries: top, bottom, left, and right. Traverse the matrix layer by layer: Left to right (top row) Top to bottom (right column) Right to left (bottom row) Bottom to top (left column) After each pass, update the boundaries. Key Insight: Carefully updating boundaries avoids revisiting elements and ensures correct traversal. Complexity: ⏱️ O(m × n) 📦 O(1) Problems like this help improve control over matrix traversal and boundary management. Special thanks to the REGex Software Services team and Prajjal Dhar Sir for continuous guidance and motivation 🙌 On to Day 32! 🚀🔥 #LeetCode #100DaysOfCode #DSA #CodingChallenge #CodeEveryday #RegexSoftware

A recurring pattern in full-stack applications: When users complain about slowness, the backend is blamed first. In practice, the real issues are often on the frontend: • heavy JavaScript bundles • avoidable re-renders • overuse of client-side state Performance is a system-level concern, not a backend-only problem. This distinction matters when building and reviewing production systems. Thoughts? X: https://lnkd.in/gM3tmD8e #FullStackDevelopment #WebPerformance #SoftwareEngineering #ReactJS

🚀 Day 31 of LeetCode Challenge | Problem 485: Max Consecutive Ones Today’s problem focused on working with binary arrays and identifying patterns efficiently 🔢 📌 Problem Overview: You are given a binary array consisting of only 0s and 1s. The goal is to find the maximum number of consecutive 1s appearing together in the array. 🧠 What this problem teaches: How to scan arrays efficiently Tracking values dynamically while iterating Importance of maintaining current state vs maximum result ✨ Why this problem is useful: Although it looks simple, it builds the habit of writing clean logic for sequence-based problems—skills that are essential for larger DSA challenges.
